## [00:00 - 03:05]

Okay, welcome to the next segment. This is going to be very, very exciting and it's going to be transformative. And by that I mean it's going to change forever the way you look at business, whether you're already started or when you're ready to start. I'm going to teach you the key to all business growth no matter what business you are in. And we talked a little bit about it but I perhaps never really developed your understanding and we're going to talk about the three way to grow a business model. And the first thing I need to tell you is that I get asked around the world, Jay, how many ways are there to grow a business? There must be a million. And my answer is always there are an infinite number of tactics. But from a strategic standpoint there are literally only three and three ways to grow any kind of business. And let me tell you what they are. Number one is increase the number of clients. Grow the number of clients, you grow the size of your business. Number two is increase the size of the transaction. If you multiply the unit of sale you tremendously multiply the profit per transaction. Number three is increase the frequency or the number of repeat purchases or the utility value meaning the other kinds of additional purchases that you can do with a buyer. Now any one of those three activities will multiply the size, the profit, the results of your business. However, if you increase all three of those continuously it has that wonderful power of geometry. It gives you the ability to compound and multiply your results by what in mathematical terms is called orders of magnitude. That's putting the geometry to work in your business. Now you should be seeing a screen right now. And the screen has a hypothetical, a simulated example of a business.

## [03:05 - 06:12]

That business for purposes of our illustration to you to make it very simple has 1,000 buyers right now, 1,000 clients. The average client is generating $100 every time they do a transaction with that business. And the average buyer is buying two times a year. So 1,000 buyers buying $100, buying two times a year means that business is doing what? $200,000 in revenue. That makes sense right? Now I should tell you almost no businesses and certainly none you're going to compete against even know those three factors. They don't even know how many active buyers they've got. They don't know how many inactive buyers they've got. They don't know what the average unit of sale is by buyer or by category, meaning different kinds of buyers have larger or smaller average orders and they don't know how often on average somebody buys. However, if you learn that using that knowledge to outrageously positive advantage is very easy. Let me demonstrate. The next slide you should be seeing shows what happens when we merely increase all three of those categories by 10%. So merely 10% increase in the number of clients you go from 1,000 to 1,100. Merely increase the size of the order from 100 to 110. Merely increase the number of purchases every year a mere 10% from two times a year to 2.2. Now even though we increase those three levels, only 10% look at the difference it makes to the total revenue. It increases at 33 and a third percent. That is the power of geometry and you have that force, that power in your hands at all time once you understand how to use it. Let's look at the last slide. We did a little more aggressive. We made one category 25% and one category 50% and one category I think 33%. But look at the difference. It's now almost 300%. Even though none of them were more than a fraction of one, the combination bumped it almost three times and when your business grows that much, with most businesses you have a very small increase in what's called your overhead, your servicing cost, meaning the

## [06:12 - 09:15]

same staff, the same facilities, the same people can handle usually a lot more sales, a lot bigger transactions. The cost for that extra increase is very low. What that means to you is that when you produce those kind of increases, your profit multiplies sometimes double, double again, even double again. The secret to great and continuous growth is to understand those three elements. Now let me explain it from a slightly different point of view. What these really are revealing to you is what's called the lifetime value of a client. That sounds a little bit sophisticated, but all it means is you need to know how much different types, kinds, and sources of buyers are worth to you. If for example somebody who comes in from a certain ad buys a certain kind of product first and then you analyze and see that they only buy three more times a year and they only buy for about a year, that tells you what that client is worth in first of all regular revenue and then you have to deduct costs and say, okay, that kind of client is worth blank profit. A different kind of client that may come from a different source or a different product may be worth five times that. They may literally buy four extra years until you know what different types of buyers, types can mean different product buyers or different sources from different media are worth. You can't know what you can afford to invest in acquiring them. And I've talked about buyers, but there's one more stage before that. Yesterday we talked a lot about, or in the last segment, we talked a lot about lead generating. But there is a value on different categories of leads. When you start generating leads or prospects or visitors to your website or opt-ins or people who come into your store, they're all variations of the concept of a lead or prospect and they have a value. They cost you something to acquire. If you run ads and your ad costs you, let's say $1,000 in a magazine or online and produces a hundred prospects, each prospect costs you $10 just to acquire.

## [09:15 - 12:18]

So you need to know that out of those hundred prospects, how many will convert or buy and what will they buy and how much profit is in what they will buy for you and how many times in a year will they buy again and how much more profit will that represent to you and approximately how many more years might they buy and how many other products might they buy because unless you know those answers, you can't know how much time, how much effort, how much attention and how much money you can afford to invest in acquiring a prospect. Then you've got to see what kind of prospects are worth more or less. A prospect from one magazine may only be worth half of what a prospect may be from another source online or a trade show. Somebody who comes in to buy a certain kind of item might be one half as valuable as somebody who comes in to buy a different kind of item because of the ongoing repeat purchases that they generate. So you've got to know those things because all leads and all sources and all buyers are not worth the same. That's why you have to analyze but the ability to analyze is your greatest gift because it will give you great profit and competitive advantage over everyone else. A couple of stories might help. I may have told it already but I'll tell it again. My very first success was ISEHOT. I think in Japan they have Tiger Bomb. ISEHOT sold for $3 a jar. It only cost us $0.55 a jar to produce. There was $2.45 profit in every jar we sold for $3. We didn't even have any money to advertise but we had the knowledge of analyzing what happened when we got 10 new buyers, first time buyers at $3 and here's what happened. Out of the 10, something like 8 bought month after month after month at $3. Of those 8, something like 4 bought additional products every month. Of those 4, something like 2 bought in big bulk so they could resell or they could give it to their friends or they could stock up. When we put everything together, the numbers showed that every time we got 10 first time buyers at $3, we were accruing or was it another word than accrue, we were creating a future

## [12:18 - 15:21]

profit stream for ourselves of approximately $50 a person every year profit for life. With that knowledge we knew we could spend a certain amount to get the first sale because we knew that every time we got 10 first sales we would get 8 repeat sales and those repeat sales would come in month after month and of the 8, 4 would buy something else and 2 would buy even something bigger. With that kind of knowledge we built it to a 500,000 buyer business. We created a product that became major distribution in drug stores and grocery stores throughout the country. Within one year we took it from a mere $20,000 to $13,000,000. We sold it for $10,000,000 to a major pharmaceutical company in less than 2 years. That's the power of this 3-way to grow a business model. Now understanding that let me make it even more exciting. You see the 3 ways. Get more clients, sell them more things, sell them more often, use them for other things. But what you don't see is that every time you add one more transactional dimension or addition to either side, the entry level first buying side or the end when they would normally stop buying it dramatically increases the profit picture to you. Let me explain why. If right now your first sale was $500 hypothetically and that eliminates a lot of people from starting a relationship with you. If you could create an entry level product that was only $100 and was 4-5th less of a deterrent to people to get started, you might get 5 or 6 times more people to start a buying relationship. With that kind of an increase, a number of them would buy the 500 and then go on to the next one and the next one. Separately, if right now you're selling single products and you could either sell larger quantities each time or you could sell higher quality upgrades each time or you could sell other products or services that you combined together as a package, you could double or redouble

## [15:21 - 18:25]

the size of the sale. And remember, doubling the size of the sale might quadruple the profit, particularly when you look at the fact that you're going to have a business, that business is going to have fixed overhead. Fixed overhead means the monthly expenses, an office, a staff, salaries, advertising. But once you get above that cost, each sale beyond that gets enormously profitable, enormously valuable, enormously powerful. Likewise, if people normally buy only a certain number of products or only a certain number of times of either the same product or your product line, if you can add additional high end products towards the end of the buying cycle, that could extend or increase the number of transactions and it would massively increase the profit. My point, without trying to confuse you, is that you must learn the three way to grow a business model because it will give you the power to multiply and control your business success and destiny. It is an unbelievably powerful tool. Every company I have grown has been grown by understanding that. Now, I said in the previous module or one before that that each of these three categories has some universal methods that you can use. Rather than going deep into them, they are fully explained in your workbook and there are a lot of different approaches. One approach is to offer a non-threatening entry level experience that costs nothing and then convert many of those prospects to buyers. Another approach is called breaking even on the front end so you can make a fortune on the back end. What that means is if you have a business that sells multiple either purchases or multiple ongoing products, psychologically this should make total sense. The sooner you get your first sale, the sooner you get your second. The sooner you get your second. The sooner you get your third. If you have to spend all the profit on the first sale in either advertising or in generous sales commissions or partnering or buying bonuses that you add to your product to make it irresistibly appealing, it doesn't matter because the sooner you get your first sale,

## [18:25 - 21:27]

the sooner you get your second. The sooner you get your second, the sooner you get your third. Your goal as an ongoing business, not a promotion because most people are just static promotions, you want to be an ongoing business, is to create recurring, sustaining, ongoing, perpetual relationships, buying relationships with your client. If your company only has a limited offering, that is not a deterrent to that goal because you can bring in other people's products and offer them to your market and retain a very large portion of the profit. You can get the rights to license other people's products or services. You can join venture. We'll get a little bit into that in a little while or into another segment, but you have enormous power. The three-way to grow a business model is critically important. We did this a little backwards because in an earlier segment, I explained the advanced three-way, so I think it's only fair to re-explain it so you understand this. I don't recommend you even go there until you master and really maximize the power of the three-way. Once you do that, you can achieve enormous success. The advanced three-way is very simple. Three different things you do every year of business. One is every year you penetrate at least one new market or one new media or one new distribution channel, one new area you haven't been in, one new way of reaching the market or one totally different market if your product can translate or be applied or adapted or adopted. That's the first thing. The second is every year you introduce at least one new product or service that you add. It can be a low end to get more people started. It can be a high end to make more profit. You decide, but every year you should do at least one, and if you're capable, multiple ones. Every year you should look at businesses you can acquire. Later in this program, not today, but I'll teach you how to buy businesses without any risk or without any capital. You should look at buying three different kinds of businesses. One is a business that is complimentary. That can mean a business that sells products or services that are purchased before, during, or after somebody buys your product or service. Second is competitive. There might be a weaker but still qualitative competitor that's struggling that you could

## [21:27 - 24:31]

buy on a pure, we call it an earn out, where you just pay the purchase price out of future profits. By consolidating, meaning by bringing their business into yours, you eliminate most of the costs that are keeping them unsuccessful. Most businesses do not fully utilize their order taking, their customer service, their support team, their delivery people, their billing department, their accounts payable, their warehouse. If you can buy other businesses and pay for them out of the profits in the future and eliminate most of their overhead but bring their buyers into your business and you do that every year, you can multiply and multiply and multiply and every time you do that, the profit explodes because the fixed overhead, the cost of managing and fulfilling and servicing those new acquired clients that you got from the business you bought is very low. You just add it to your existing staff. The other thing to buy is a related business. I use yesterday or whenever you watch this and I'm recording this over a series of days, I talked about a supplement, a weight supplement. I said that you were directly competing if you sold a supplement for weight loss with other supplement sellers. But you're indirectly competing with gyms, personal trainers, books, videos, equipment, portion control food. Most people trying to lose weight will bounce back and forth through all of those things. If you own related businesses, you could source clients from that business over to yours and you could direct clients from your business over to that.
